title_en: A top oligarch breaks his silence
title_zh: 一位顶级寡头打破沉默
rubric_en: An interview with Andrey Melnichenko on the future of Russia
rubric_zh: 安德烈·梅尔尼琴科谈俄罗斯未来——访谈纪要

paragraphs.en: The clock on the Kremlin tower had already struck midnight on May 28th 2025 when Andrey Melnichenko was finally summoned. The building was largely empty but its most important occupant was still awake. Vladimir Putin greeted his guest and invited Melnichenko to sit beside him, offering him tea. “Move closer, Andrey,” Putin said kindly. Melnichenko’s gaze landed on a red folder which lay in front of Russia’s president—he guessed it was a dossier about him supplied by the security services.
paragraphs.zh: 2025年5月28日午夜刚过，克里姆林宫塔楼上的时钟已经敲响午夜。安德烈·梅尔尼琴科终于被传唤进来。大楼里几乎空无一人，但最重要的那位主人仍旧清醒。弗拉基米尔·普京迎接了这位来客，并邀请梅尔尼琴科坐到他身边，递给他一杯茶。“坐近一点，安德烈。”普京和善地说。梅尔尼琴科的目光落在总统桌前的一只红色文件夹上——他猜想那是安全部门提供的、关于他的资料袋。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko, a slim, tall man with a gawky bearing, is one of Russia’s richest men. He had met Putin before in the company of other Russian businessmen. But this was the first time he had asked to see nachalstvo (“the leadership”) one on one. He began by introducing himself. Over the past 35 years he has built up an industrial empire with vast holdings in fertilisers, mining, energy and logistics. Yet he had always kept a low profile. Even Putin seemed surprised to hear about the scale of Melnichenko’s operations, which account for nearly 1% of Russia’s GDP.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科是俄罗斯最富有的人之一，身材高瘦、略带有些生涩的笨拙感。他过去曾与其他俄罗斯商界人士一起见过普京，但这是他第一次主动提出与“nachalstvo（领导层）”一对一会面。他先是自我介绍。过去35年里，他建立起了一个庞大的工业帝国，在化肥、采矿、能源和物流领域拥有巨大的业务布局。然而他一直保持低调。即使是普京本人听到他事业规模后也似乎感到意外——那几乎占到俄罗斯GDP的1%。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko began to talk about his public role, including the brief on climate policy he held with the Russian Union of Industrialists and Entrepreneurs, a club of businessmen. Putin began to lose interest. Melnichenko thought he could read a question in Putin’s eyes: “Why are you here? What do you want from me?”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科开始谈论自己在公开场合的角色，其中还提到了他与俄罗斯工业联络企业家联盟的一次气候政策简报会——这是一个由企业家组成的俱乐部。普京开始逐渐失去兴趣。梅尔尼琴科似乎从普京的目光中读到一个问题：“你为什么会来？你到底想从我这里得到什么？”

paragraphs.en: “Is someone bothering you?” Putin asked.
paragraphs.zh: “有人在烦你吗？”普京问道。

paragraphs.en: “Vladimir Vladimirovich, honestly, I understand that people usually come to you for one of two reasons. Either they’re afraid, there’s some problem. Or they want something—a position, a favour…As for fear, of course I’m afraid, 100%, like everyone else. Why deny it? I’m not afraid of anything specific, unless there’s something in your folder. But if there is, please tell me so at least I know what to fear.”
paragraphs.zh: “弗拉基米尔·弗拉基米罗维奇，老实说，我理解人们通常来找你有两个原因。要么是害怕，有问题；要么是想要点什么——一个职位、一个照顾。说到害怕，当然我也害怕，百分之百，和别人一样。为什么要否认呢？我并不害怕任何具体的事，除非你文件里真的有什么。要是有，请你告诉我，至少让我知道该害怕什么。”

paragraphs.en: Putin smiled. Melnichenko’s directness had grabbed his attention.
paragraphs.zh: 普京微笑了。梅尔尼琴科的坦率抓住了他的注意。

paragraphs.en: “As for ambition,” Melnichenko continued, “my ambition was to build a global fertiliser company, one of the largest in the world, operating in every market, independent of any one country…I wanted to build a global company simply because it was interesting.”
paragraphs.zh: “至于野心，”梅尔尼琴科接着说，“我的野心是建立一个全球化的肥料公司，成为世界上最大的公司之一，进入每一个市场，不依附于任何一个国家……我想建立一家全球公司，仅仅因为这件事很有意思。”

paragraphs.en: But then “all this happened”—by which he meant the war with Ukraine, the sanctions, the unpredictability of geopolitics. “The world turned out not to be what I thought. There is no global world. There are no global rules…You understood it before we did.”
paragraphs.zh: 但后来“这一切发生了”——他指的是与乌克兰的战争、制裁以及地缘政治的不可预测性。“我发现世界并不是我想象的那样。没有一个真正全球化的世界。没有全球规则……你们比我们更早明白这一点。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko, who was placed under sanctions in the first days of the war, told Putin how he was forced to hand his wife ownership of EuroChem, then the world’s second-largest fertiliser firm.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科是在战争最初几天被列入制裁名单的人之一，他告诉普京自己被迫将自己妻子列为欧罗化学（EuroChem）法人，欧罗化学是当时世界第二大化肥公司。

paragraphs.en: “Who is your wife?” Putin enquired.
paragraphs.zh: “你妻子是谁？”普京问道。

paragraphs.en: “She is a Serb,” Melnichenko told him.
paragraphs.zh: “她是塞尔维亚人。”梅尔尼琴科对他说。

paragraphs.en: “Well, Serbs are decent, honest people,” Putin said. “They won’t steal it.”
paragraphs.zh: “塞尔维亚人很正派、很诚实，”普京说，“他们不会把它据为己有。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko then turned to the purpose of the visit. He wanted to become more involved in public life and to work for the good of his country. The mistake of the elite, Melnichenko told Putin, had been to separate their own fate from the fate of Russia. The supposed guarantee by the West of their property rights had nearly cost them their fortunes. “You cannot have one country for making money and another for security, your family’s future and real life,” said Melnichenko, and thanked the Russian leader for helping him see the light.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科随后谈到了这次访问的目的。他希望更积极地参与公共生活，并为国家的利益而工作。梅尔尼琴科对普京说，精英们的错误在于把自己的命运与俄罗斯的命运分离开来。西方对其财产权的“保障”几乎让他们失去了全部财富。“你不能有一个国家赚取财富，却在另一个国家寻求安全、为自己的家庭和真实生活寻找未来，”梅尔尼琴科说，并感谢俄罗斯领导人帮助他看清了真相。

paragraphs.en: Putin was pleased. For years he had warned Russian oligarchs that they were not truly welcome in the West. Now, one of the foremost among them had admitted that he was right. The meeting lasted over an hour, far longer than Melnichenko had expected.
paragraphs.zh: 普京很高兴。多年来他一直警告俄罗斯寡头，他们在西方并不真正受欢迎。如今，这些寡头中最前列的一位承认他的判断是正确的。会面持续了一个多小时，远远超过了梅尔尼琴科预期的时间。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko spent just five minutes telling me about his visit to the Kremlin during nearly 60 hours’ worth of conversations we have had over the past three months. He wouldn’t give any more details of the meeting with Putin, but he did share, with startling frankness, the story of his own rise, his diagnosis of the existential challenge that Russia faces and his vision for the country’s future which could change Russian history if he manages to realise it.
paragraphs.zh: 在我们过去三个月几乎六十小时的交谈中，梅尔尼琴科只花了五分钟向我提到他访问克里姆林宫的事情。他不愿透露更多关于与普京会面的细节，但他坦率地讲述了自己崛起的经历，分析了俄罗斯面临的生存性挑战，并表达了他对国家未来的构想；如果他能实现，这一构想可能会改写俄罗斯的历史。

paragraphs.en: He is nothing like most businessmen whose ambition leads them to believe they can run for office. He arrived at our first meeting in Istanbul after a 28-day fast to purify his body and mind. He does not pontificate. Rather, I could see the effort he put into wrestling his thoughts into words, using his entire body to draw them out. He seemed interested in using dialogue to test and shape his ideas.
paragraphs.zh: 他与那些认为自己的雄心足以竞选公职、并将其一视同仁的企业家截然不同。第一次在伊斯坦布尔见面时，他刚刚完成了28天的禁食，以净化身心。他不喜欢夸夸其谈。相反，我能看出他下了功夫，把零散的想法打磨成可表达的文字，动用了全身去整理和表达。看来他很重视通过对话来检验并塑造自己的想法。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko studied quantum physics at university, and he carries with him the discipline’s seemingly contradictory impulses. He thinks in systems like a physicist. But he thrives on the fundamental uncertainty of the world around him. Each of those traits is useful and together they have propelled him to the top of global business. They may yet equip him to play a role in restructuring Russia and its place in the world.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科在大学里学习量子物理，因此他身上带着这门学科看似矛盾的冲动。他思考问题时像物理学家一样以系统化方式展开，但又能在周围世界的根本不确定性中找到自己的节奏并加以利用。这两种特质都很有价值，也正是它们共同把他推到了全球商业顶端。它们甚至可能使他有能力参与重塑俄罗斯及其在世界中的地位。

paragraphs.en: As we talked, Melnichenko’s ideas took shape. Our conversations inspired him to write an essay laying out his vision of Russia’s future, which The Economist is publishing online this week. At the heart of that vision is the idea of sovereignty—not just of the state but also of the elites and citizens who, under Putin, have been excluded from participation in their country’s politics.
paragraphs.zh: 在交谈中，梅尔尼琴科的想法逐渐成型。我们的交流激发他撰写了一篇文章，阐明他对俄罗斯未来的设想，该文本周由《The Economist》在线发布。该构想的核心是“主权”——不仅是国家的主权，也包括在普京统治下被排除在国家政治参与之外的精英和普通公民的主权。

paragraphs.en: The security establishment sees any independence as a threat. An outsider taking the initiative—a rich businessman no less—may be perceived as a direct challenge. Melnichenko is well aware of the dangers. “Any move beyond silent compliance poses risk,” he told me. “It would be natural to keep one’s head down. But the stakes are too high and if you don’t participate, you exclude yourself from your own future.”
paragraphs.zh: 安全机构将任何独立性都视作威胁。一个局外人抢先发声——哪怕是一个富商——也可能被看作直接挑战。梅尔尼琴科深知其中的危险。“任何超出沉默顺从的举动都伴随着风险，”他对我说，“低调做人本是很自然的。可代价太高，如果你不参与，就等于把自己排除在自己的未来之外。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko is a latecomer to Russian statecraft. Despite being the richest man in Russia when Putin invaded Ukraine in 2022, he is not a household name inside or outside the country. For the past 20 years, he has treated Russia as a foreign investor would, flying in for a few weeks a year to inspect his businesses. The rest of the time he either lived in Switzerland or cruised round the world on his yacht. This was partly because he wanted to live like other international billionaires. He has cultivated relationships with tech titans such as Larry Ellison, the leaders of the UAE and the court of Muhammad bin Salman, the crown prince of Saudi Arabia.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科在俄罗斯国家事务方面是后来者。尽管在2022年普京入侵乌克兰时他是俄罗斯最富有的人，但在国内外他都不是家喻户晓的人物。过去20年里，他把俄罗斯当作一位外国投资者来对待，每年只飞去几周去视察自己的企业。其余时间他要么住在瑞士，要么驾着游艇周游世界。这在某种程度上也是因为他想过上其他国际亿万富豪的生活方式。他已与科技巨头如拉里·埃里森、阿联酋领导层以及沙特王储穆罕默德·本·萨勒曼的宫廷建立了关系。

paragraphs.en: But it’s also because of the agreement Putin struck with the oligarchs when he came to power: stay out of politics and you can keep your fortunes and enjoy your life abroad. The country was instead dominated by the siloviki, alumni of the security services.
paragraphs.zh: 但这也与普京上台时与寡头们达成的交易有关：别介入政治，你就能保住自己的财富，在海外过上想要的生活。否则，国家就会被以安全部门为背景的“siloviki”（军警情报体制出身的精英）所主导。

paragraphs.en: When Putin invaded Ukraine, the world waited for Russia’s rich and powerful to speak against the war. But they stayed quiet. The West imposed sanctions on them, partly to make them apply pressure on Putin. But this betrayed a lack of understanding of how power worked in Russia—the business elite had long given up trying to influence politics.
paragraphs.zh: 当普京入侵乌克兰时，世界期待俄罗斯的富豪和权贵站出来公开反对这场战争。但他们保持了沉默。西方对他们实施了制裁，部分目的就是希望他们向普京施压。然而，这暴露出西方对俄罗斯权力运行方式缺乏理解——这个商业精英阶层早已放弃了试图影响政治的愿望。

paragraphs.en: Putin himself was worried the oligarchs would betray him. Instead sanctions pushed them back into his embrace. Yet when they returned, they repatriated their interests and ambitions along with their money. Melnichenko’s opening gambit when our conversations began three months ago was: “For the first time I feel I have no other country but Russia.” Given the reticence of his peers, it is astonishing that Russia’s most enigmatic oligarch, while living in Moscow, is willing to put his head above the parapet and lay his views on the record.
paragraphs.zh: 普京本人也担心寡头们会背叛他。结果，制裁反而把他们重新推回到他的怀抱。不过，当他们回来的时候，连同财富一起，也把他们的利益和野心也“归国”了。我们三个月前开始交谈时，梅尔尼琴科的开场白是："我第一次感到，自己除俄罗斯外，再没有别的国家可归属。"鉴于同侪的大多沉默，令人惊讶的是，俄罗斯这位最神秘的寡头如今居住在莫斯科，却愿意挺身而出，把自己的观点公开记录下来。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko is not a dissident. He is neither an anti-war hero nor a pro-war zealot. The businesses he runs help sustain the war economy. Ammonia produced by his plants is used in munitions. He works with the armed forces to defend his factories from drone attacks and co-operates with the security services to avert sabotage. He avoids ideological and moral judgments, because he thinks they are a distraction from the task of designing a system that works.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科并不是异见人士。他既不是反战英雄，也不是亲战争狂热分子。他经营的企业实际上在支持战争经济。其工厂生产的氨用于弹药制造。他与武装力量合作，保护自己的工厂免受无人机袭击，并与安全部门协作以防止破坏行动。他刻意回避意识形态和道德判断，因为他认为这些会分散精力，而这些判断会干扰去设计一个能运转的制度。

paragraphs.en: He is not seeking to challenge the existing power structure. Rather, he is offering his services to the government and hopes to become an architect of change. The course of his career suggests that he is not interested in wielding direct control. He served as a non-executive director on the boards of his companies, at times allowing his chief executives to overrule him. His skill has been in bringing together disparate interests to construct systems that transform natural resources: building ports and railways, synthesising fertiliser, generating electricity. Hard-won mastery over the physical world, he believes, gives him a clear-eyed perspective that ideologues lack.
paragraphs.zh: 他并不是想去挑战既有的权力结构。相反，他是在向政府提供自己的服务，并希望自己能成为推动变革的设计者。以他的职业经历来看，他对直接掌握权力并不感兴趣。他曾在自己公司的董事会担任非执行董事，有时还会允许首席执行官推翻他的意见。他擅长的是把彼此分化的利益方聚拢在一起，搭建改造自然资源的体系：建设港口和铁路、合成化肥、发电。梅尔尼切科夫认为，经过长期实践而获得的对现实世界的驾驭能力，让他能比意识形态者更清醒地看待问题。

paragraphs.en: The war with Ukraine has now gone on longer than Russia’s involvement in the second world war. Melnichenko is stepping up as Moscow finds itself under military assault for the first time since 1942. Ukraine’s attacks on Russia’s refineries has caused a massive fuel shortage. Drones are striking industrial and military facilities deep inside Russia, stripping the Kremlin of its aura of invincibility. The economy is under strain. For some time, elites have felt that the country has reached a tupik—a dead end. Now, the arrival of war on the home front has added a fresh layer of discontent.
paragraphs.zh: 与乌克兰的战争现在持续的时间已经超过俄罗斯参与第二次世界大战的时间。梅尔尼切科夫在莫斯科军队第一次在 1942 年以后遭遇国内正面军事打击的背景下，正逐步站到前台。乌克兰对俄罗斯炼油厂的袭击引发了大规模燃料短缺。无人机正打击位于俄罗斯纵深的工业和军用设施，正在剥离克里姆林宫“无可战胜”这一光环。经济也在承压。相当长一段时间里，精英阶层一直觉得国家已经走到了一个“T字路口”——一条死胡同。现在，战争已经来到本土，又在上面加了一层新的不满。

paragraphs.en: This means the conflict may escalate dangerously. “The war in Ukraine is unequivocally seen as a war between Russia and the West,” said Melnichenko. He thinks there is a worryingly high chance that it will spill over into other countries. He does not rule out the use of nuclear weapons. That is an outcome he is desperate to avoid. “Whatever the reasons that brought us here—whether these people were right or those people were wrong—we have to recognise the reality: we’re now in uncharted territory.”
paragraphs.zh: 这意味着冲突可能会危险升级。梅尔尼切科夫说，“乌克兰战争无可争辩地被视为俄乌与西方之间的战争。”他认为这场冲突有一个令人担忧的高概率会蔓延到其他国家。他并没有排除核武器被使用的可能性。这是他急切想要避免的结果。“无论把我们带到今天的是哪些原因——不管是谁对还是谁错——我们都必须承认现实：我们如今已经进入了无人先例的领域。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko is not scared of chaos—he sees it as a laboratory to test himself as a builder of systems. His fortune was created as modern Russia emerged from the carcass of the Soviet Union. He has always been a step ahead of change, identifying value which is underpriced. Today there is a heightened demand for order, predictability and an idea of a future. “People are not afraid of freedom. People are afraid of chaos,” Melnichenko said. From the outside, the regime seems impregnable. Yet that had also seemed the case in 1913 and in 1986. Those governments fell a few years later.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼切科夫并不害怕混乱——他把它视为检验自己建构体系能力的试验场。他的财富是在现代俄罗斯从苏联遗骸中崛起时形成的。他始终走在变化前沿，擅长发现被低估的价值。今天，人们对秩序、可预期性以及对未来的设想需求格外强烈。“人们并不害怕自由，人们害怕的是混乱。”梅尔尼切科夫说。从外界看来，这个政权似乎坚不可摧。然而在1913年和1986年，这种印象也曾一度存在。那些政权几年前后都倒台了。

paragraphs.en: It is important to understand Melnichenko’s story for two reasons: first, because he is likely to become a force who cannot be ignored; second, it shows him to be a meticulous strategist, who plans patiently and acts decisively. So far, he has always got what he wants.
paragraphs.zh: 理解梅尔尼琴科的故事有两个重要原因：其一，他很可能会成为一个不容忽视的力量；其二，他的经历显示他是一个谨慎的战略家，能够耐心布局并果断行动。到目前为止，他一直如愿以偿。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko was born in 1972 in Gomel, the second-largest city in Belarus, into a family of Soviet intelligentsia. His father was a physicist and a semi-professional basketball player; his mother, an ethnic Ukrainian, taught Russian language and literature in a local music school.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科于1972年出生在白俄罗斯第二大城市戈梅利，出生于一个苏联知识分子家庭。其父亲是一名物理学家，也是半职业篮球运动员；其母亲是乌克兰裔，曾在当地一所音乐学院教俄语和文学。

paragraphs.en: In the spring of 1986, when Melnichenko was 14, the nuclear plant blew up in Chernobyl, 160km from Gomel. He experienced Chernobyl as both a catastrophe and a liberation. He and his classmates were evacuated to an empty military base in Lithuania that had recently stored short-range nuclear missiles. “It was the freest and most interesting summer of my childhood: no parents, no school supervision, total freedom. You could crawl through missile silos, many of them not sealed up yet, wander through underground passages: it was risky, exciting and girls liked it.”
paragraphs.zh: 1986年春天，梅尔尼琴科14岁时，距离戈梅利160公里的切尔诺贝利核电站发生了爆炸。对他来说，切尔诺贝利既是一场灾难，也是一种解放。那时，他和同学们被疏散到立陶宛一处空置的军用基地，这里最近刚存放过短程核导弹。“那是我童年里最自由、最有趣的夏天：没有父母，没有学校监管，完全自由。你可以爬进导弹发射井——很多还没有封闭起来——穿行于地下通道：既危险又刺激，而且女生很喜欢去。”

paragraphs.en: Chernobyl was also a catalyst of glasnost (openness) in the Soviet Union. As attempts to conceal the scale of the disaster failed, the pervasive culture of secrecy and fear began to melt away. Magazines started to publish previously banned writing about Stalin’s repressions, “I watched teachers quite literally rewrite their interpretation of history on the fly.” Foreigners and goods were starting to trickle over the Soviet border. As a teenager, Melnichenko would buy Western clothes and electronics from Poles and Hungarians and sell them in street markets in Gomel.
paragraphs.zh: 切尔诺贝利也成了苏联“glasnost”（公开性、透明化）浪潮的催化剂。随着试图掩盖这场灾难规模的努力失败后，社会上普遍存在的保密和恐惧文化开始瓦解。杂志开始刊登过去被禁的有关斯大林大清洗的文章。“我亲眼看到老师们几乎是在当场改写他们的历史观。”外国人和外国商品也开始从苏联边境慢慢渗入。作为一名十几岁的少年，梅尔尼琴科会向波兰人和匈牙利人买来西方服装和电子产品，再拿到戈梅利的街头市场上出售。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko was clever and placed in a stream for advanced maths and physics. At 15 he went to the leading Soviet school for mathematics, which was attached to Moscow State University (MSU). Unlike most other parts of the Soviet education system, it was genuinely meritocratic. Students who did well, like Melnichenko, studied physics at MSU. Others trained as cryptographers at the KGB academy.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼钦科聪明过人，被分流到一个专门学习高等数学与物理的课程。15岁时，他进入苏联最顶尖的数学学校就读，这所学校附属于莫斯科国立大学（MSU）。与苏联教育体系的其他多数领域不同，这里的选拔机制实际上是按才能定人的。像梅尔尼钦科这样的成绩优秀学生能够在MSU学习物理，其他人则在克格勃学院训练成为密码学家。

paragraphs.en: His studies at MSU shaped Melnichenko’s perception of the world. He specialised in quantum statistics and field theory, which deals with sub-atomic particles and their behaviour. “In the quantum world, nothing is certain and everything is probable. Reality is fluid,” he explained. “If something disproves your view of life—some event, phenomenon, whatever—it is not a tragedy, it is an opportunity. I’m not rigid. Tomorrow I may believe something different from what I believed the day before yesterday.” This mentality was perfect for a country in upheaval.
paragraphs.zh: 在MSU的学习经历塑造了梅尔尼钦科对世界的认知方式。他专攻量子统计和场论，这门学科研究的是亚原子粒子及其行为。他解释道：“在量子世界里，没有什么是确定的，一切都是概率，现实是流动的。”“如果某个事件、现象——无论什么东西——否定了你对生活的看法，那不是悲剧，而是一个机会。我不是固执的人。明天我可能会相信与前天完全不同的东西。”这种思维方式非常适合一个正在剧变的国家。

paragraphs.en: In the spring of 1989 he attended political rallies and rock concerts by Western bands such as the Scorpions, whose song “Winds of Change” became an anthem of freedom. He and a friend bought tickets for Pink Floyd’s gigs via employees at theatre box offices—prices were still set by the state—and resold them through a network of students at a huge mark-up.
paragraphs.zh: 1989年春天，他参加了政治集会，也去看了西方乐队的摇滚演唱会，比如“Scorpions”的演出，而他们的《Winds of Change》那首歌已经成了自由的颂歌。他和一位朋友通过剧院售票窗口的工作人员买到平时由国家统一定价的平克·弗洛伊德演出票，然后通过一个学生网络高价转售。

paragraphs.en: At MSU, Melnichenko met a woman called Larisa, his first love. “She was beautiful, intelligent and she was five years older.” Larisa had studied music and sang jazz. Though he was certain she loved him back, Larisa suddenly disappeared in the spring of 1990. Later, one of her friends told Melnichenko that she had married a rich Muscovite and was expecting a child.
paragraphs.zh: 在MSU，梅尔尼钦科认识了一个叫Larisa的女孩，这是他的初恋。“她很漂亮，也很聪明，而且比我大五岁。”Larisa学过音乐，会唱爵士乐。尽管他确信她也爱他，但Larisa在1990年春天突然消失。后来，她的一位朋友告诉梅尔尼钦科，她已经嫁给了一位有钱的莫斯科人，并且怀上了孩子。

paragraphs.en: He was devastated but there was a twist in the tale. “Two years after her wedding, I met Larisa again. By then I was a different person. I had money, status, confidence.” He resumed the relationship—but only because he wanted to hurt her. Melnichenko got his revenge. “It was probably the most shameful thing I did in those years, and I can find no justification for it.”
paragraphs.zh: 他几乎崩溃了，但故事却出现了转折。“结婚两年后，我又见到拉丽莎。那时的我已是另一个人。我有了钱、有了地位、有了自信。”他重新开始了这段关系——但仅仅是因为他想伤害她。梅尔尼琴科于是报了复仇。“那可能是我那几年做过的最丢人的事，我想不出任何可以为此辩护的理由。”

paragraphs.en: The story of Larisa taught him two lessons: revenge, however tempting it may be, humiliates the perpetrator more than the target. The second was that Moscow was obsessed with money.
paragraphs.zh: 拉丽莎的故事给了他两课：报复再有诱惑力，最终羞辱的往往是施报者，而不是被报仇的人。第二课是，莫斯科人对金钱有一种执迷。

paragraphs.en: The collapse of the command economy and rapid impoverishment of the population created an enormous appetite for magic shows and gambling as people sought a miracle. Card games, shell games and lotteries, run by con artists, sprung up everywhere. Melnichenko and a friend sensed an opportunity. “We observed it, rationalised parts of it, simplified it rather cynically, and basically made it efficient.” Melnichenko’s game favoured the house, but it did allow people to win—he realised that for the business to endure he needed his customers to share in the benefits.
paragraphs.zh: 计划经济的崩溃和民众快速贫困化之后，人们把赌局和魔术表演当成寻求奇迹的出口，导致这类活动需求巨大。纸牌戏法、壳牌游戏和彩票在骗子的操盘下到处出现。梅尔尼琴科和一位朋友看到了机会。“我们观察了这一现象，对其中一些环节作了理性解读，带着相当冷嘲的色彩把它简化，并且基本上让它变得高效。”梅尔尼琴科的游戏偏向庄家，但他也让玩家有赢面——他意识到，要想让这项业务持久下去，就必须让顾客也分享到收益。

paragraphs.en: The game was simple. The player drew ten sticks from a set of 60. Each stick was marked with a number from one to ten, and the total was then checked against a payout table to determine whether they had won. “We made sure our operators stuck to the rules. The hope we were selling was genuine.” This increased the game’s popularity, even though “I understood who was profiting from whom.”
paragraphs.zh: 这是一种很简单的玩法。玩家从60根小棍中抽出10根。每根上都标着1到10的数字，然后把总和与赔率表核对，判断是否中奖。“我们确保自己的工作人员严格按规则来。我们兜售的希望是真实的。”这让这项游戏更受欢迎，尽管“我明白了谁在从谁那里赚得更多。”

paragraphs.en: To avoid getting shut down, Melnichenko needed to square the authorities. He acquired permits to operate the lottery by pretending it was a charitable foundation. (Later it was registered as a charity—the cause supported was named as “strengthening relationships with those who issued permits”.)
paragraphs.zh: 为了避免被叫停，梅尔尼琴科不得不先与当局“对齐”。他通过伪装成慈善基金会来取得经营彩票的许可。（后来它正式注册为慈善组织——所支持的事业被写成“加强与颁发许可者的关系”。）

paragraphs.en: A far bigger problem was thugs shaking down businesses. To prevent this “injustice”, Melnichenko relied on a friend whose father headed a police academy in Volgograd. “Why negotiate with gangsters when there are plenty of half-trained policemen around?” Hiring them made paying off a protection racket unnecessary and the cops also helped to manage the crowd “which may become disappointed with its financial result and punch you in the face”.
paragraphs.zh: 更大的问题是地痞流氓敲诈企业。为了防止这种“冤枉事”，梅尔尼琴科依靠了一位朋友，这位朋友的父亲曾是伏尔加格勒警校的负责人。“既然周围有这么多半训练的警察，为什么还要跟黑帮谈判呢？”雇佣他们后，支付保护费就不再必要，而且警察也能帮助维持人群秩序，毕竟这群人“可能会对其财务结果感到失望，并当场揍你一拳”。

paragraphs.en: Through his business, Melnichenko acquired a network that has risen along with him. Many of the MSU students who operated the lottery became government officials and businessmen who are still in his orbit. The police cadets now occupy senior positions in the security services.
paragraphs.zh: 凭借他的生意，梅尔尼琴科逐步建立起一张与其共同成长的人脉网络。许多当年在彩票项目中工作的莫斯科州立大学学生后来成为了政府官员和商人，至今仍在他的影响范围内。那些警校学员如今也占据了安全部门的高位。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko got another student to manage day-to-day operations while he went to lectures and “thought about things”. This was the role he has always preferred. “I was always a leader but almost never in the foreground.” His low-key style was honed at the summer camps he had attended as a child, where the strongest boys were the most popular. “The moment you try to advance your own agenda, however admirable it may be, you inevitably provoke the desire of some less reflective but physically stronger boy…to punch you in the eye.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科另派了一名学生来负责日常经营，以便他去上课并“思考一些问题”。这正是他始终偏好的角色。他说：“我一直是个领袖，但几乎从不站在前台。”他在童年参加夏令营时形成了这种低调风格——那里，最强壮的男孩往往最受欢迎。“你一旦试图推进自己的议程，无论目标多么可贵，都不可避免地会激起某个缺乏思辨但体格更强壮的男孩——他想要揍你一拳。”

paragraphs.en: He learned how to be diplomatic. “I have never liked violence as a way of achieving an objective. The best option is to attract people by proposing objectives they either had not seen themselves or did not believe were attainable.”
paragraphs.zh: 他学会了如何圆滑处世。“我一直不喜欢把暴力当成达成目标的手段。最好的办法是通过提出别人要么没想到、要么认为无法实现的目标来吸引他们。”

paragraphs.en: By the end of his first year of university, Melnichenko was driving a Lada. By the end of his second year, he had upgraded to a Mercedes. He had 40 lottery stands operating across Moscow. The lottery generated a steady flow of roubles, but these were losing value by the day as inflation shot up. He converted his earnings into dollars—then an activity that was still illegal outside official channels. He obtained these through Chinese and American students who had access to greenbacks through expat connections. These contacts turned into friendships—one became a senior official in the Chinese government and his son heads Melnichenko’s Shanghai office. The American became a congressman. Any excess dollars he had were sold to other aspiring entrepreneurs. He opened his first informal currency exchange in a dorm room at MSU.
paragraphs.zh: 大学第一年结束时，梅尔尼琴科开着一辆拉达。到了第二年末，他升级为奔驰轿车。他在莫斯科经营着 40 个彩票摊点。彩票业务带来稳定的卢布收入，但由于通胀飙升，这些卢布的购买力每天都在下降。他将收入换成美元——那时这在非正式渠道外仍属违法行为。为了弄到美元，他借助有留学生关系的中国和美国学生。后来这些联系人都成了朋友：其中一个后来成为中国政府高级官员，他的儿子如今负责梅尔尼琴科在上海的办事处；那位美国人则成为了一位国会议员。他把多余的美元卖给其他有创业野心的人。他在莫斯科国立大学的一间宿舍里开设了他的第一个非正式外汇兑换点。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko spent the summer of 1991 in Crimea. Not far away, Mikhail Gorbachev was being detained by the KGB in a last-ditch attempt to preserve the Soviet Union. Melnichenko sensed that these were the death throes. He felt “neither fear nor euphoria—just a sense of an inevitable process accelerating”.
paragraphs.zh: 1991 年夏天，梅尔尼琴科在克里米亚。没走多远，米哈伊尔·戈尔巴乔夫正在被克格勃拘押，苏联当局做着最后一次挽救联盟的努力。梅尔尼琴科感觉到这一切正在走向终结。他说自己“既没有恐惧，也没有狂喜，只觉得一场不可逆转的过程正在加速”。

paragraphs.en: When he returned to Moscow, he did something unthinkable a few weeks earlier. Betting that the old system was hollow, he advertised his illegal foreign-currency exchange in a newspaper. The risk paid off and the business grew rapidly. He finally dropped out of university and folded the lottery.
paragraphs.zh: 当他回到莫斯科时，做了一件几周前还难以想象的事。押注旧体制已经名存实亡后，他在报纸上公开刊登了非法外汇兑换的广告。高风险带来了回报，生意迅速扩大。后来他终于辍学，并且关闭了彩票生意。

paragraphs.en: What he wanted now was to make his operations official—not easy if you’re a 20-year-old without government connections. What he did have, though, was chutzpah and a knack for winning the trust of older men of his grandfather’s generation. He had two guardian angels. One was David Kruk, a 70-year-old war veteran who ran Premier, a co-operative bank. Melnichenko found him through the yellow pages and made Kruk an offer. Premier would receive a fixed fee for bringing his exchanges under its roof; any additional profits would be kept off the books.
paragraphs.zh: 他现在想要做的是把自己的业务弄得合法化——对于一个20岁、又没有政府关系网的年轻人来说这并不容易。可他手里有的，是胆大包天的魄力，以及赢得祖辈那一代年长男性信任的本事。为此他有两位“守护天使”。其中一位是大卫·克鲁克，这位70岁的战争老兵掌管着普雷米尔，一家合作银行。梅尔尼琴科是在黄页上找到他的，并向克鲁克提出了一个提议：普雷米尔可收取固定费用，帮助承接他的外汇交易；任何超出的利润都可以不入账。

paragraphs.en: After six months Melnichenko poached Kruk’s staff and set up on his own, but he managed to stay friends with his mentor until Kruk’s death at the age of 96. He called the new bank Moskovsky Delovoy Mir (Moscow Business World, or MDM for short).
paragraphs.zh: 六个月后，梅尔尼琴科挖走了克鲁克的员工并独立开办了自己的业务，但他始终和这位导师保持着友好关系，直到克鲁克96岁去世。随后他将新银行命名为“莫斯科德洛沃伊米尔”（Moscow Business World，简称MDM）。

paragraphs.en: Meanwhile, Russian politics was breaking down. In 1993 the country suffered a constitutional crisis when communists and ultra-nationalists led an insurgency against Boris Yeltsin, the president. There was shooting on the streets. Melnichenko picked up a gun and headed out—not to get involved but to observe.
paragraphs.zh: 与此同时，俄罗斯的政治体系正在瓦解。1993年，国家经历了一场宪政危机，反对派中的共产党人和极端民族主义者联合发动了反对总统鲍里斯·叶利钦的武装起义。街头出现了枪战。梅尔尼琴科拿起枪走了出去——不是去参战，而是去旁观。

paragraphs.en: To consolidate his position in foreign-currency dealing, Melnichenko needed to find a regular supplier of dollars. His other guardian angel was an American banker called Richard Spikerman, the head of international relationships at the Republic National Bank of New York, the main importer of American bank notes into Russia.
paragraphs.zh: 为了巩固他在外汇交易中的地位，梅尔尼琴科需要找到一个稳定的美元供给来源。他另一位守护天使是美国银行家理查德·斯派克曼，后者是纽约共和国国民银行的国际关系负责人，也是向俄罗斯进口美钞的主要机构。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko won him over. “We stood outside in the snow and grilled steaks. I was 60. He was 22. He reminded me of my son,” said Spikerman, who worked with him up until last year. Now Melnichenko had a direct line that bypassed the official Russian exchanges.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼切恩科赢得了他的信任。“我们站在雪地里烤牛排。我那时60岁，他才22岁，他让我想起了自己的儿子，”斯皮克曼说道，他一直在梅尔尼切恩科身边工作到去年。现在，梅尔尼切恩科有了一条绕过俄罗斯官方交易所的直接通道。

paragraphs.en: Republic National would fly up to $300m-worth of notes a day from vaults in Europe to Moscow. Melnichenko arranged the transport and security for his share. He rented a disused nuclear shelter near Moscow for storage. Trucks drove two storeys underground, where dozens of people sat counting cash.
paragraphs.zh: 共和国国家银行（Republic National）每天会从欧洲的金库运送价值高达3亿美元的现金票据到莫斯科。梅尔尼切恩科安排了他那部分款项的运输和安保。他在莫斯科附近租下了一处废弃的核防护掩体作仓储。卡车会开到地下两层，在那里，几十个人坐着清点现金。

paragraphs.en: “Russia in the 1990s was an engine of acceleration: it destroyed people and created them at the same pace,” Melnichenko said. Rules were flexible and success or failure depended on one’s agility. “If you could think a little faster than others and formulate things a little better, things worked out for you.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼切恩科说：“20世纪90年代的俄罗斯是一台加速引擎：它会摧毁人，也会在同样速度下创造人。规则很灵活，成败取决于个人的敏捷度。‘只要你反应得比别人快一点，表达得更清楚一点，事情就会对你有利。’”

paragraphs.en: In 1992 the government began the biggest privatisation in history. Many of Melnichenko’s friends became owners of companies that were previously state-owned; he served as their banker. His big clients included the Chernoy brothers, who assembled a metals empire with the help of political connections and underworld muscle. The turf war they fought over Russia’s aluminium left hundreds dead in the smelter cities of Siberia.
paragraphs.zh: 1992年，政府启动了有史以来规模最大的私有化。梅尔尼切恩科的许多朋友成为原本属于国家企业的所有者；他充当了他们的银行家。他的重要客户包括切诺伊兄弟，他们依靠政治关系和黑社会势力，组建起一个金属王国。他们围绕俄罗斯铝业展开的地盘争夺战，让西伯利亚多个冶炼城市数百人丧生。

paragraphs.en: In 1996 the Chernoy brothers’ empire fell. They were succeeded by a select group of tycoons, who were handed control of Russia’s natural resources by Yeltsin’s government in return for political support. After Yeltsin’s re-election in 1996, Boris Berezovsky, one of the most prominent of these oligarchs, declared, “now we have the right to occupy government posts and enjoy the fruits of our victory.” Melnichenko was not of their number. He observed from the side, as they scrapped with each other to expand their personal fiefs instead of investing their wealth and energies in improving the public realm.
paragraphs.zh: 1996年，切诺伊兄弟的帝国崩溃了。接替他们的是一小撮寡头，这些人是在叶利钦政府给予政治支持后，换取对俄罗斯天然资源控制权的人。1996年叶利钦连任后，这些寡头中最知名的鲍里斯·别列佐夫斯基曾宣布：“现在我们有权进入政府职位，并享受我们胜利的果实。” 梅尔尼琴科不在他们之中。他在一旁观察着他们彼此争夺、互相撕扯，去扩大各自的私有封地，而不是把财富和精力投入改善公共领域。

paragraphs.en: In 1998 Russia defaulted on its debt and devalued the rouble. Many of the oligarchs were exposed and lost vast sums of money. Melnichenko, who had converted his earnings into foreign currency, came away not just unscathed but stronger. As other Russian banks went under, he scooped up some of the country’s biggest firms as clients. One of these was owned by Roman Abramovich, who had worked his way into Yeltsin’s inner circle. “He made a big impression on me. For the first time, I saw a strategist—disciplined, patient and systematic.”
paragraphs.zh: 1998年，俄罗斯违约并贬值卢布。许多寡头被迫现出原形并损失了巨额财富。梅尔尼琴科把自己的收入换成外币，因此他不但没有受损，反而变得更强。随着其他俄罗斯银行倒闭，他收购了该国一些最大企业作为客户。其中一家属于罗曼·阿布拉莫维奇，他曾进入叶利钦核心圈子。“他给我留下了很深印象。那是我第一次看到一位真正的战略家——有纪律、有耐心，而且做事系统。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko “came to understand how power in Russia really worked”. It did not derive from official positions of authority, he realised, but ran through informal networks, where only trust and loyalty mattered. Melnichenko saw how Abramovich had built up contacts everywhere—from regional governors to Yeltsin’s family—and wielded influence without displays of dominance. “People who made up his ‘collective’ did not realise they were part of a single plan…that existed in his head. Abramovich seemed to be nowhere, yet soon he was everywhere. From a certain moment”, around spring 1999, “there were no oligarchs in Russia. There was just Abramovich.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科“逐渐明白了俄罗斯的权力究竟如何运作”。他意识到它并不来自正式的权力职位，而是存在于非正式网络之中，在那里只有信任和忠诚才算数。梅尔尼琴科看到，阿布拉莫维奇在各处建立了关系网——从地方州长到叶利钦家族——并且不靠霸道的表面姿态就能施加影响。“组成他‘集体’的人并不知道自己其实是他心中那张单一棋盘的一部分……阿布拉莫维奇似乎无处不在却又不显露其身影，但很快他就无处不在。大约从1999年春天起，“俄罗斯已经没有寡头了。这里只有阿布拉莫维奇。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko did not want to be part of someone else’s system, nor was he interested in politics. Instead, he wanted to create an industrial empire. In 1999 he hired Alexander Mamut, a plugged-in figure, as chairman of his bank. “Within a year I got to know absolutely everyone who one needed to know.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科既不想成为别人体系中的一员，也不热衷于政治。相反，他想要打造一个工业帝国。1999年，他聘请了与体制关系紧密的亚历山大·马穆特担任其银行的董事长。“一年之内，我几乎把所有该认识的人都认识了。”

paragraphs.en: Abramovich was instrumental in managing the transition from Yeltsin to Putin. Melnichenko also recognised this marked the end of an era. He offered hundreds of thousands of dollars to lure opposition candidates to Putin’s party and received a letter of gratitude from Putin himself after he was elected president in 2000.
paragraphs.zh: 阿布拉莫维奇在叶利钦向普京的权力更替过程中起到了关键作用。梅尔尼切科也意识到，这标志着一个时代的结束。他向反对派候选人提供了数十万美元，以吸引他们加入普京的政党；在普京于2000年当选总统后，他本人还收到了普京的感谢信。

paragraphs.en: Early that year Melnichenko went to the Himalayas. “I wanted to think everything through, calmly.” He had money, political connections and a clear goal: to consolidate some of the largest industrial resources in the country. He had his eye on three sectors: making metal pipes, making fertiliser and mining coal. “Everything was part of a unified strategy.”
paragraphs.zh: 当年年初，梅尔尼切科前往喜马拉雅山。“我想把一切想清楚，冷静思考。”他拥有资金、政治关系和清晰的目标：整合国家里一些最大的工业资源。他瞄准了三个行业：生产金属管道、生产化肥和采煤。“这一切都属于一个统一的战略。”

paragraphs.en: Each target was, on the face of it, unpromising. Export prices for Russia’s commodities were in the basement, agriculture was in a dire state and domestic tariffs for electricity and gas were set artificially low. Many domestic consumers simply did not pay at all, safe in the knowledge that they would not be cut off.
paragraphs.zh: 表面上看，每个目标都前景黯淡。俄罗斯商品的出口价格跌到谷底，农业处于十分严峻的状态，而国内的电力和天然气关税被人为地定得过低。许多国内消费者干脆不付账，因为他们知道不会被停电或停气。

paragraphs.en: But Melnichenko had a hypothesis: that China’s economic growth would create new demand for coal, and require food from North and South America, which would therefore need fertilisers. Internal reforms would establish fair energy tariffs, and consumers would begin paying in full. Rising oil and gas prices would create domestic demand for pipes. And scrap metal, the raw material for making them, was cheap and plentiful.
paragraphs.zh: 但梅尔尼切科有一个假设：中国的经济增长会带动煤炭需求，并需要来自南北美洲的粮食，因此需要化肥。国内改革将建立合理的能源关税，消费者会开始全额缴费。油价和天然气价格上涨会带动对管道的国内需求。废钢，这一制造它们的原料，价格便宜而且供应充足。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko started with coal: sweeping up undervalued mines, and connecting them to railway hubs, ports and power generators. In the east of the country, he built an export terminal at the end of an underutilised railway line—a 2,700-mile-long (4,345km) Soviet mega-project that ran from Siberia to the Pacific.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科从煤炭起步：拿下被低估的矿山，并将其与铁路枢纽、港口和发电厂连接起来。在国家东部，他在一条利用率低下的铁路线上修建了一个出口码头——这条从西伯利亚通向太平洋、全长2,700英里（4,345公里）的苏联巨型工程此前一直没有得到充分利用。

paragraphs.en: The main consumers of coal inside the country were electricity producers. Anatoly Chubais, the architect of privatisation, had been appointed in 1998 as head of RAO UES, the electricity monopoly, with a mission to break up one of the last remaining pillars of the centralised economy and promote competition. Melnichenko paid a call to introduce himself. “I believe that your reform will work out,” he told him.
paragraphs.zh: 国内煤炭的主要消费方是发电企业。安纳托利·丘拜斯，这位特许私有化的主要设计者，于1998年被任命为国家电力垄断企业RAO UES的负责人，任务是拆解这一中央集权经济的最后支柱之一并推动竞争。他来了个拜访，向他自我介绍。“我相信你的改革会成功，”他当时对丘拜斯这么说。

paragraphs.en: Chubais was used to dealing with bolshy minority shareholders or oligarchs demanding favours. The man visiting him was so polite that “he looked like a schoolboy walking into the staff room during break,” Chubais recalled. There was one more thing, Melnichenko added. “We raised $2.5bn and we bought 15% of your shares.”
paragraphs.zh: 丘拜斯已经习惯了与要求特殊待遇的强势小股东或寡头打交道。那位来访者礼貌得体，以至于丘拜斯回忆道：他“看起来像个在课间走进办公室休息室的小学生”。梅尔尼琴科又补充说了件事。“我们筹集了25亿美元，买下了你公司15%的股份。”

paragraphs.en: Chubais was so stunned he thought he had misheard. This would make Melnichenko the largest individual investor in RAO UES. Chubais waited for the new owner’s demands. None came. As Melnichenko prepared to leave, Chubais could not help asking: “Why did you come, Andrey? Do you have any wishes?” “No, I just came to inform you,” Melnichenko replied. Then he left. For the next seven years, he did not make a single request. “It was my dream come true,” Chubais said. “He was a new kind of industrialist.”
paragraphs.zh: 丘拜斯当时惊得几乎怀疑自己听错了。这将使梅尔尼琴科成为RAO UES最大的个人投资者。丘拜斯等着看新股东会提出什么要求。却一个也没有。梅尔尼琴科准备离开时，丘拜斯还是忍不住问道： “你来这里干什么，安德烈？你有任何诉求吗？” “没有，我只是来告知你这件事，”梅尔尼琴科回答道。然后他就离开了。接下来的七年里，他没有提出过任何要求。丘拜斯说：“这是我梦寐以求的。” “他是一个新型企业家。”

paragraphs.en: It took just two years for Melnichenko, whose name had previously been barely known outside business circles, to emerge as one of Russia’s top industrialists. “Everything was done very quickly. And nobody could understand what we were doing. Only once everything was finished did it become clear.” In 2002 Nezavisimaya Gazeta, a Russian newspaper, quoted one Western businessman who said of Melnichenko, “He’s 30 years old, and he wants to rule the world.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科只用了两年，便从一个在商业圈之外几乎鲜为人知的名字，成长为俄罗斯最顶级的实业家之一。“一切进展得非常快。没人能理解我们在做什么。只有当一切完成后，才变得清晰。”2002年，俄罗斯报纸《独立报》引用一位西方商人的话说梅尔尼琴科：“他才30岁，却想统治世界。”

paragraphs.en: By 2004 his plan was complete. MDM Group had merged 50 enterprises into three companies: the Pipe Metallurgical Company (TMK), EuroChem, a mineral and chemical company, and the Siberian Coal Energy Company (SUEK). The power plants served Russia, his coal exports were sent to Asia and EuroChem looked West. He had, as his old friend Spikerman said, become “a globalist by design”.
paragraphs.zh: 到2004年，他的计划已经完成。MDM集团将50家企业并入三家公司：管道冶金公司（TMK）、EuroChem（一家矿产与化工公司）和西伯利亚煤炭能源公司（SUEK）。这些发电厂为俄罗斯提供电力，他的煤炭出口销往亚洲，而EuroChem则将市场看向西方。正如他的一位老友Spikerman所说，他已经成为“天生的全球主义者”。

paragraphs.en: While Melnichenko was consolidating his industrial assets, Putin and the security services were establishing control over the elites and eliminating independent power bases. The new leader sidelined most of the oligarchs. In 2003 he arrested Mikhail Khodorkovsky, the politically ambitious owner of Yukos, Russia’s largest oil firm, and seized his company.
paragraphs.zh: 当梅尔尼琴科在整合他的工业资产时，普京和安全部门正在对精英实施控制并清除独立的权力基础。新领导人把大多数寡头排除在外。2003年，他逮捕了政治抱负很高、拥有尤科斯的米哈伊尔·霍多尔科夫斯基，后者是俄罗斯最大石油公司尤科斯的所有者，并没收了他的公司。

paragraphs.en: Moguls were told that “you don’t need to be involved in anything other than your business,” Melnichenko recalled. “In fact, you are explicitly expected not to be involved.” He did not mind this arrangement and neither did his peers. By the early 2000s they were looking outwards. “The same impulse that had once led me from Gomel to Moscow was now pushing me to New York, London and Paris,” said Melnichenko.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科回忆道，财阀们被告知：“你不需要参与自己企业以外的任何事务。” “事实上，你被明确要求不去参与。”他并不介意这种安排，他的同辈也同样不在意。到2000年代初，他们的目光已经向外转移了。“曾经把我从戈梅利推到莫斯科去的那股力量，如今又把我推向纽约、伦敦和巴黎。”梅尔尼琴科如是说。

paragraphs.en: Part of this shift was his decision to get married. He approached his personal life as systematically and deliberately as his business affairs—like “Chinese dynasties. There was no randomness in it, absolutely none.” He chose his future wife before he actually met her.
paragraphs.zh: 这次转变的一部分是他决定结婚。正如他处理商业事务一样，他也很有条理、很有计划地安排个人生活——“像中国王朝一样。里面没有任何随机性，绝对没有。”他在真正见到她之前就已经选好了未来的妻子。

paragraphs.en: Aleksandra Nikolić, known as Sandra, was five years younger than him. She had been raised in the former Yugoslavia. Her father was a Serbian architect, her mother a Croatian artist. As the most popular singer in the Balkans and a model with a degree in international management, she was beautiful, famous and clever. She was also his entré into European society.
paragraphs.zh: Aleksandra Nikolić，大家都叫她Sandra，比他小五岁。她在前南斯拉夫长大。她的父亲是塞尔维亚建筑师，母亲是克罗地亚艺术家。她既是巴尔干地区最受欢迎的歌手，也是拥有国际管理学位的模特，外貌出众、名气很大，而且聪明伶俐。她也成为他进入欧洲社会的“入口”。

paragraphs.en: At their first meeting in Antibes, Melnichenko told her straight away he was planning to marry her. She was taken aback, but he was confident. “The moment I saw her, I knew that everything I’d heard about her before was true—and much more besides. I fell in love with her immediately.”
paragraphs.zh: 在他们于安提布的第一次见面时，Melnichenko直接告诉她他打算娶她。她很惊讶，但他很坚定。“我一看到她，就知道我之前听到的关于她的一切都是真的，而且远不止这些。我当场就爱上了她。”

paragraphs.en: Six months later they moved in together and, two years after that, they got married. The wedding cost $30m. The 300 guests were fed by Alain Ducasse, a Michelin-starred chef, and entertained by Whitney Houston, Christina Aguilera and Enrique Iglesias.
paragraphs.zh: 六个月后他们开始同居，之后两年，他们结婚了。婚礼花费了3,000万美元。300位嘉宾由米其林星级厨师 Alain Ducasse 负责用餐，Whitney Houston、Christina Aguilera 和 Enrique Iglesias 为他们表演。

paragraphs.en: By the time of their wedding, Melnichenko was no longer a Russian tax resident. In 2004 he moved to France, and later to Switzerland, and stepped back from the day-to-day operations of his companies. A life apart suited him. “I had worked very hard, essentially, since childhood. I just wanted to live…I had no interest in improving the world, let’s put it that way.”
paragraphs.zh: 到他们结婚时，梅尔尼琴科已经不再是俄罗斯税务居民。2004年他搬到了法国，后来又去了瑞士，并且逐步退出了自己公司的日常运营。这种分开的生活很适合他。“我几乎从童年开始就非常努力地工作。我只想要好好生活……如果这么说的话，我并没有兴趣去改善世界。”

paragraphs.en: Until then his knowledge of Western business was mostly theoretical. There was only one way to turn it into practice: “start living the way the people you aspire to become actually live, spend time how they spend it, see how decisions are really made.”
paragraphs.zh: 在那之前，他对西方商业的了解几乎只停留在理论上。要把理论变成现实，只有一个办法：“过你想成为的人真正过的生活，花时间去过他们的生活，看看决策是如何真正做出的。”

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko commissioned a yacht from the designer Philippe Starck, whom he gave complete freedom. It was 119 metres long and weighed 6,000 tonnes. The prow was shaped like the beak of an eagle and it glided stealthily through the sea with barely a ripple, “like a whale”. The boat, called A after his and his wife’s shared initial, set sail in 2008. It served as his calling card and an expression of life unconstrained by borders or nationality. He surveyed the globe, spending eight months a year on board. “We were building a global Russia integrated into the world economy and Western institutions.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科委托设计师Philippe Starck为他打造了一艘游艇，并给予了他完全的创作自由。游艇长119米，重达6,000吨。船首像鹰喙一样，被描述为在海面上几乎无声滑行，“像一头鲸鱼”。这艘以他和妻子共同首字母命名为“A”的船于2008年下水。它既是他的名片，也象征着一种不受国界和国籍限制的生活方式。他环游世界，每年在船上度过八个月。“我们正在建设一个与世界经济和西方机构融合的全球化俄罗斯。”

paragraphs.en: From the deck of his yacht he began devising a global investment strategy. In 2011 he conceived a plan to spend $35bn over 15 years, picking up assets across the world, and building new plants in Russia. “I did not receive much in the way of dividends, I was investing in the long term.” The plan succeeded—but the world for which it was made would fall apart.
paragraphs.zh: 从游艇甲板上，他开始制定全球投资战略。2011年，他设想出一项为期15年的计划，准备投资350亿美元，收购全球各地资产，并在俄罗斯建设新的工厂。“我几乎没有收到多少分红，我是在进行长期投资。”这项计划最终成功了——但它所针对的世界却开始崩解。

paragraphs.en: Two conflicting tendencies began to emerge inside Russia in 2008, the year that Putin took a four-year hiatus as president. Following years of growth and high oil prices, the country was modernising socially and economically. The first post-Soviet generation was coming of age. They wished to be citizens rather than subjects and were receptive to international influences.
paragraphs.zh: 2008年，普京卸任总统、开始休整四年之际，俄罗斯内部开始显现出两种相互冲突的趋势。经过多年增长和高油价之后，这个国家在社会和经济层面都在进行现代化。第一代后苏联一代人已逐渐步入成熟。他们希望自己是“公民”而不是“臣民”，并且愿意接纳国际影响。

paragraphs.en: But Putin, who continued to wield power as prime minister, realised integration with the West would upend Russia’s political system. This would undermine his power and that of the siloviki, and his desire to recreate the Soviet empire by asserting a Russian sphere of influence. His return to the Kremlin in 2012 was greeted with mass protests by Russia’s middle class. At that point he made a deliberate choice to set the country on a path of confrontation.
paragraphs.zh: 但普京在仍以总理身份继续掌握权力的同时意识到，融入西方将会颠覆俄罗斯的政治体制。这不仅会削弱他个人的权力，也会削弱军警安全集团（siloviki）的权力，同时也会冲击他试图通过重建俄罗斯势力范围来复兴苏联帝国的愿望。2012年他重新回到克里姆林宫时，遭到俄罗斯中产阶层的大规模抗议。此时，他有意选择了让国家走向对抗的道路。

paragraphs.en: While Melnichenko was expanding his horizons, Putin began to isolate Russia and restore the symbols of empire. None was more potent than Crimea, which he annexed in 2014. Sanctions followed as Putin began to turn Russia into a fortress—self-reliant, hardened against pressure, its population on a leash.
paragraphs.zh: 当梅尔尼琴科在不断拓展自己的视野时，普京开始把俄罗斯与世界隔离，并重建帝国象征。没有什么比2014年他吞并克里米亚更有力度。随着普京将俄罗斯打造成一个要塞——自给自足、在压力面前硬化、民众被紧紧控制——制裁随之而来。

paragraphs.en: That year Melnichenko moved the headquarters of EuroChem to Switzerland, where he lived. He did not recognise the magnitude of the tensions between Russia and the West. He saw crises in business terms, where matters are resolved when the costs grow too high. “I didn’t think it would go as far as it did. It shouldn’t have gone that far.” Melnichenko now sees his error. “While I was continuing to build an international company, the world in which such companies could exist was already coming to an end.”
paragraphs.zh: 就在那一年，梅尔尼琴科将其EuroChem总部迁往瑞士，并在那里居住。他没有意识到俄罗斯与西方之间紧张局势的严重程度。他用商业逻辑看待危机：在成本高到让人无法承受时，问题就会得到解决。“我没想到局势会发展到那一步，也不应该发展到那一步。”梅尔尼琴科现在承认了自己的错误。“在我继续打造一家国际化企业的时候，这种能够让跨国公司生存的世界早已走向终结。”

paragraphs.en: Then in 2022 his world imploded. On the day that war broke out, Melnichenko was in Moscow to attend a long-scheduled engagement with Putin, alongside other Russian business leaders. Putin projected confidence. “Unfortunately, our meeting is taking place in unusual conditions, to put it mildly, but we planned it in advance,” he told the stunned gathering. None of those present had expected the Russian president to pull the trigger. They had persuaded themselves that war couldn’t happen because it would be a disaster. Nonetheless, none of them spoke against it.
paragraphs.zh: 然后，2022年，他的世界崩塌了。战争爆发的当天，梅尔尼切夫在莫斯科，与其他俄罗斯商业领袖一道出席一场与普京早就安排好的会面。普京表现得很有信心。“坦白说，我们这次会面发生在异常的条件下，但它早已提前安排好了。”他对震惊的与会者们这样说。与会者无人预料到俄罗斯总统会按下扳机。大家都自我说服，战争不可能发生，因为那将是一场灾难。尽管如此，他们谁也没有公开反对。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko was put under sanctions on March 9th. The EU claimed that this was because he was part of “the closest circle of Vladimir Putin”. EuroChem itself was exempted from sanctions, so as not to endanger the world’s food supply. Melnichenko later challenged the sanctions in the European Court of Justice, but his arguments were dismissed.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼切夫于3月9日被列入制裁名单。欧盟声称这是因为他属于“弗拉基米尔·普京的最亲近圈子”。欧元区并未将欧姆化学（EuroChem）本身列入制裁名单，以免危及世界粮食供应。梅尔尼切夫后来在欧洲法院挑战这些制裁，但他的辩护理由被驳回。

paragraphs.en: He had already taken countermeasures. On March 8th, his 50th birthday, he resigned as the beneficiary of the trusts that held assets used by the family—houses, planes, yachts—and transferred them to Sandra, an EU citizen who had never lived in Russia and had no Russian passport. A few days later the Italian government seized his newest yacht. The EU bureaucracy was not his only source of trouble. Business partners in Europe tried to exploit his predicament, he says.
paragraphs.zh: 他已经提前采取了反制措施。3月8日，也就是他五十岁生日那天，他辞去了家族资产信托的受益人身份，将这些信托所持资产——房产、飞机、游艇——转让给桑德拉，一位从未在俄罗斯居住、也没有俄罗斯护照的欧盟公民。几天后，意大利政府没收了他的最新游艇。欧盟官僚体系并不是他唯一的麻烦。欧洲的商业伙伴也想借他的困境牟利，他是这样说的。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko’s initial strategy was to sit out the war and wait for things to return to normal. “There was a period in 2022-23 when I tried to play by the old rules because it seemed to me that this whole story was temporary.” He based himself in Dubai and made friends with the Emirati elite. The tipping point came in a conversation with Tucker Carlson, then a MAGA tribune, in a hotel in Dubai in the build-up to the 2024 American election. “It left me with a clear impression that if Trump wins, he would embrace fragmentation.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼切夫最初的策略是对战争“局外观望”，等待局势恢复常态。“在2022年至2023年间，我确实有一段时间试着按旧规则行事，因为在我看来，这一整件事会暂时过去。”他把基地放在迪拜，并与阿联酋精英结交。转折点出现在一次与塔克·卡尔森（当时的“占领美国”阵线代言人）的会谈中。当时他在迪拜一家酒店与卡尔森交谈，正值2024年美国大选前夕。“这次谈话给我留下了明确印象：如果特朗普赢了，他会推崇‘碎片化’。”

paragraphs.en: At around that time he and other businessmen realised that the war would not end soon. With no reprieve from sanctions in sight, they started to repatriate themselves back to Russia, where they faced a different kind of threat to their assets.
paragraphs.zh: 就在那时，他和其他商人意识到战争不会很快结束。由于看不到制裁缓解的迹象，他们开始把自己迁回俄罗斯，在那里他们面临着对资产的另一种威胁。

paragraphs.en: Property rights in Russia had always been conditional. But the war unleashed a rapacity that hadn’t been seen for decades. Since 2023, assets worth $60bn have been nationalised or handed over to loyalists. It was the biggest redistribution of property since the mass privatisations of the 1990s.
paragraphs.zh: 在俄罗斯，财产权一直是有条件的。但战争释放出的掠夺性是几十年来前所未见的。自2023年以来，价值600亿美元的资产已被国有化或移交给亲俄派。这是自1990年代大规模私有化以来，规模最大的财产重新分配。

paragraphs.en: In August 2023 prosecutors sought to confiscate Sibeco, a Siberian power plant, from Melnichenko, arguing that his purchase of it had involved fraudulent collusion with the previous owner. Two weeks later, the prosecutor-general’s office backed down in return for Melnichenko making a donation to a “charitable organisation”. According to people familiar with the settlement, the sum was 32bn roubles ($335m)—the same amount that Melnichenko had originally paid for Sibeco. The charitable organisation was Sirius, a school for gifted children favoured by Putin.
paragraphs.zh: 2023年8月，检察机关试图没收梅尔尼琴科持有的西伯利亚电厂Sibeco，理由是他购入该电厂时与前任所有者存在欺诈性串通。两周后，检察长办公室在梅尔尼琴科向一家“慈善组织”捐款后退出了追诉。熟悉这起和解的人士称，金额为320亿卢布（3.35亿美元）——正是梅尔尼琴科最初购买Sibeco时支付的同样数额。该慈善组织是Sirius——一家受普京青睐的天才儿童学校。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko now knew that he needed to establish property rights in Russia. The only way to do this was to work his way into the system, understand the competing interests and help shape its objectives. “If you want a seat at the table, you have to do something.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科现在明白，他需要在俄罗斯建立起资产的财产权。做到这一点的唯一途径，是融入体制，理解不同利益之间的博弈，并帮助塑造其目标。“你若想坐到桌前，就得出点力。”

paragraphs.en: As always he began by observation. “In 2023 I started to spend more time in Russia and was getting to know it in a much deeper way.” He talked to anyone who had a stake and a viewpoint: “politicians, journalists, thinkers, liberals, nationalists, communists”. He could be found having breakfast with Dmitry Muratov, the Nobel prizewinner and founding editor of Novaya Gazeta, a liberal newspaper, who was ostracised by the government and branded a “foreign agent”. In the evening he might have tea with Alexander Dugin, a nationalist philosopher who glorifies the war.
paragraphs.zh: 他一如既往地从观察开始。“2023年我开始在俄罗斯待得更多，开始更深入地认识这个国家。”他与任何有利益关系、也有观点的人交谈：“政治家、记者、思想者、自由主义者、民族主义者、共产主义者”。他可能会和德米特里·穆拉托夫一起吃早餐——这位诺贝尔奖得主、自由派报纸《新报》创始主编，因为受到政府排斥并被贴上“外国代理人”标签；晚上，他又可能和亚历山大·杜金共进茶点，这位民族主义哲学家在讴歌战争。

paragraphs.en: Conversation by conversation, he found a way to imagine what Russia ought to become. “Everybody has different interests, but there is a consensus that a clear idea of the future has been lost.” To much of the world, no serious discussion of Russia’s future is possible until after the war. To Melnichenko, the end of the war in Ukraine is contingent on imagining that future—which hardly anyone in either Russia or the West is prepared to do.
paragraphs.zh: 他每次对谈都在推演中寻找俄罗斯应该成为什么样。 “每个人都有不同的利益，但达成共识的是：对未来的清晰认知已经丢失了。” 对世界上许多人而言，除非战争结束，否则几乎不可能认真讨论俄罗斯的未来。对梅尔尼琴科来说，乌克兰战争的结束取决于对这一未来的想象——而几乎没有人，无论在俄罗斯还是在西方，愿意去做这件事。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko’s vision is set out in his online essay. It is not ideological. He does not think in terms of democracy and autocracy, good and evil, friend and enemy, defeat and victory. His most important goals are building consensus, restoring stability and working for Russia’s long-term interests.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科的设想写在他发表在网上的一篇文章里。它并非意识形态化。他不以民主与专制、善与恶、朋友与敌人、失败与胜利来思考问题。他最重要的目标是形成共识、重建稳定，并为俄罗斯的长期利益而努力。

paragraphs.en: He explained to me four scenarios, all of which he thinks are grim. In the first, Russia is brought in from the cold and reintegrated into the Western order while being confined to its periphery. To Melnichenko that sounds like vassalage; he thinks a humiliated and defeated Russia would almost certainly generate aggressive revanchism.
paragraphs.zh: 他向我解释了四种情景，他认为全部都很严峻。第一种是俄罗斯被从“寒冷”中带回并重新并入西方秩序，但仅限于其边缘地位。在梅尔尼琴科看来，这听起来像是附庸地位；他认为，一个被羞辱并战败的俄罗斯几乎必然会滋生激进的复仇情绪。

paragraphs.en: A second is for Russia to be permanently drawn into China’s orbit as a buffer zone and supplier of natural resources. China may have little interest in this scenario: it would be responsible for Russia’s problems and face a backlash from humiliated Russians. “China is happy to benefit from asymmetry in the relationship but is in no hurry to formalise it,” he argues. To him, the only difference between these two scenarios is the identity of the feudal overlord.
paragraphs.zh: 第二种情况是俄罗斯被永久拖入中国的轨道，成为一个缓冲区和天然资源供应国。中国可能对这种情况兴趣不大：这意味着它将承担俄罗斯的问题，并且要面对受挫的俄罗斯人可能引发的反弹。梅尔尼琴科认为：“中国乐于从这段不对称关系中获益，但并不急于将其正式化。”在他看来，这两种情形唯一不同之处在于封建宗主的身份。

paragraphs.en: The two other scenarios are more alarming for being entirely plausible. Russia could descend into civil conflict with different warlords vying for scarce resources. A fragmenting country with a nuclear arsenal was America’s worst nightmare when the Soviet Union collapsed and it remains so today. “A broken Russia may be an attractive image in political speeches,” Melnichenko says. “It is far less attractive from the standpoint of risk management.”
paragraphs.zh: 另外两种情形更让人警惕，因为完全可能发生。俄罗斯可能陷入内战，不同军阀为争夺有限资源而厮杀。一个核武库在手、正在分裂的国家，是苏联解体时美国的噩梦，如今仍然如此。梅尔尼琴科说：“在政治演讲中，一个四分五裂的俄罗斯也许是个很有吸引力的画面。” “但从风险管理的角度看，它远远不那么吸引人。”

paragraphs.en: The fourth scenario, promoted by the security services and subject to serious discussion in the Kremlin, poses a direct threat to Melnichenko and the Russian elite: a North Korea-style lockdown sustained by militarism, repression, isolation, rationing and the export of instability. “A Russia trapped in the mentality of a permanent fortress would transform external confrontation into a permanent instrument of domestic politics,” he says.
paragraphs.zh: 第四种情景由安全部门推崇，并在克里姆林宫内被认真讨论，对梅尔尼琴科和俄国精英构成直接威胁：一种像北韩一样的封闭局面，由军国主义、镇压、孤立、配给制和向外输出不稳定支撑。梅尔尼琴科说：“一个被困在永久要塞心态中的俄罗斯，会把外部对抗转变为国内政治的常态化工具。”

paragraphs.en: The only alternative to these scenarios, as Melnichenko sees it, is for Russia to become what he calls a “sovereign” country, one that places the comfort of its people first and whose behaviour is predictable to the outside world. The word “sovereignty” has had varying meanings in Russian politics over the past three decades. Yeltsin invoked sovereignty to show that the new democratic country was distinct from the Soviet Union. One astute Russian philologist thought the idea was absurd. “Who is Russia seeking sovereignty from, the polar bears?” she wrote in her diary.
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科认为，这些情景唯一的替代方案，是让俄罗斯成为一个他称之为“主权”的国家，即把民众的福祉放在首位，并且让其行为对外部世界可预期。过去三十年里，“主权”一词在俄罗斯政治中有不同含义。叶利钦曾借“主权”来表明新民主国家与苏联的区别。一位敏锐的俄罗斯语文学者认为这个念头荒唐。她在日记中写道：“俄罗斯究竟要从谁那里寻求主权？是北极熊吗？”

paragraphs.en: Putin, who argued that modern Russia shared characteristics with the Soviet Union and the Russian empire before it, invoked sovereignty to justify the domination of the state over its people. Sovereignty became a synonym for Russia’s resurgence as a great power—one opposed to the Western liberal order. It was also a byword for Putin’s personal rule and his right to impose his will on former Soviet republics.
paragraphs.zh: 普京认为当代俄罗斯与苏联及其前身的俄罗斯帝国有相似之处，并以“主权”为由证明国家对其人民实行统治的合理性。“主权”因此成了俄罗斯作为大国复兴的代名词——一个与西方自由主义秩序相对立的大国。它同时也成了对普京个人统治的代称，以及他有权对前苏联加盟共和国施加意志的依据。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko uses the word “sovereignty” differently: to refer to the agency of the elites with a stake in the country. Citizens too “may come to understand the importance of agency as well—provided they are offered not a simulation of participation but an opportunity to discuss real alternatives honestly”. A country’s greatness, Melnichenko argues, has nothing to do with sloganeering. It depends on whether it protects citizens’ freedom and property “not as an act of good will but as an obligation”.
paragraphs.zh: 门尼切科使用“主权”一词时有不同含义：他指的是那些对国家有利益攸关的精英的能动性。公民也“同样可以理解能动性的重要性——前提是，他们得到的不是参与的假象，而是诚实讨论现实选择的机会”。门尼切科认为，一个国家的伟大与口号无关。它取决于国家是否“将保护公民的自由和财产”视为“一种善意施舍”还是“应尽的义务”。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko is convinced that the challenges that Russia faces are so severe that wider participation is needed. Businessmen, leaders of civil society, nationalists, technocrats ought to get involved. These people have different, sometimes opposing interests but would agree on a compromise not because they like each other, but because alternatives would be worse. Such a polity would be an oligarchy in the classical sense of the word—rule by elites. The “internal debate about what Russia should become is unavoidable—and it will be intense.”
paragraphs.zh: 门尼切科确信俄罗斯面临的挑战十分严峻，因此需要更广泛的参与。商人、民间社会领袖、民族主义者、技术官僚都应当参与进来。这些人拥有不同、甚至彼此对立的利益，但他们会在妥协上达成一致，不是因为彼此喜欢对方，而是因为可行替代方案会更糟。这样的政治形态将是按经典含义理解的寡头政治——由精英统治。“关于俄罗斯应成为什么样子的内部讨论是不可避免的，而且会十分激烈。”

paragraphs.en: The main aim is to make the country “a place where people are drawn to because they genuinely like it, because life there is better: safer, more secure, more predictable, more prosperous—and because they feel they can realise themselves.” For Melnichenko, attracting people from former Soviet republics and tempting back those who fled the country is the only way of making Russia economically viable.
paragraphs.zh: 其核心目标是把国家建设为“一个让人愿意前来、因为他们真的喜欢它的地方，因为那里生活更好：更安全、更有保障、更可预期、更富足——并且因为人们在那里能实现自我。”在门尼切科看来，要让俄罗斯在经济上可持续发展，唯一办法就是吸引来自前苏联加盟共和国的人才，并把那些离开的人重新吸引回来。

paragraphs.en: This vision is strikingly close to the one advocated by Tsar Fedor Ioannovich—the son of Ivan the Terrible and the titular hero in one of Russia’s most popular history plays: “We should make Russia so pleasant that people would not prefer living abroad. Then there will be no reason for them running away from us.”
paragraphs.zh: 这一愿景与沙皇费奥多尔·伊万诺维奇——伊凡雷帝之子，也是俄罗斯最受欢迎的历史戏剧之一《铁证在身》中的主角——所倡导的想法惊人地接近：“我们应当把俄罗斯建设得足够宜居，让人们不愿选择去国外定居。那样他们就没有理由离开我们。”

paragraphs.en: “This Russia does not have to be convenient to the West or to China,” Melnichenko argues. “It has to care primarily about its own citizens and be predictable and rational to the outside world. It might be a difficult partner to the West, but it is not a black hole. It might be less amenable to China than a client state, but it is safer than a large and volatile vassal.”
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科认为：“这个俄罗斯不必对西方或中国‘方便’。它首先要关心的是本国公民，并且对外部世界保持可预期、理性的态度。对西方来说，它可能是一个困难的合作伙伴，但绝不是黑洞。它对中国的可利用程度可能不如一个附庸国，但比一个巨大而动荡的附庸国更安全。”

paragraphs.en: His proposals are self-serving, obviously. He clearly wants Russia to become a place where there is a role for him. If the country turns into North Korea, his children will not have a future there. If it descends into civil war, his fortune will not survive.
paragraphs.zh: 他的主张显然带有明显的自利性。他显然希望俄罗斯成为一个他仍有位置的国家。如果这个国家变成了北韩，他的孩子们在那儿就没有未来。如果国家走向内战，他的财富也无法幸免。

paragraphs.en: It is far too early to say if Melnichenko’s idea of Russia’s sovereignty can become reality. The odds against it are high: veterans returning from the front will increase social tensions; the frustrations of a population that feels it has been put under a state of emergency for no benefit are already blatant. The security services will not give up power easily and they are so universally despised that collaboration with them may not be possible.
paragraphs.zh: 目前说梅尔尼琴科的“俄罗斯主权”构想是否会成为现实还为时过早。反对这种设想的障碍很大：从前线返乡的退伍军人将加剧社会紧张；一部分民众对自己被置于紧急状态却看不到收益的失望已经十分明显。情报安全机构不会轻易放弃权力，而且它们受到了普遍厌恶，与其合作可能并不现实。

paragraphs.en: Yet Russian history rarely follows a script. There is certainly a yearning among the elite for someone to say what everybody already thinks. It might only take one man to break the silence for the floodgates to open. And the lesson of the past is that epochal shifts begin at the top. As Yegor Gaidar, Russia’s former prime minister said, big changes in Russia happen later than we think, but earlier than we expect.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，俄罗斯历史很少按既定剧本展开。精英阶层中确实有一种渴望，想听到一个人说出大家其实都已经在想的话。或许只需要一个人打破沉默，闸门就会随即打开。过去的经验告诉我们，划时代的转折往往始于上层。正如俄罗斯前总理叶戈尔·盖达尔所说，俄罗斯的重大变化往往发生得晚于我们以为，却早于我们预料。

paragraphs.en: Melnichenko knows this, too. He told me that “the moment of change cannot be predicted. There are moments in history that just happen. Trying to accelerate is usually a mistake. All you can do is observe and be ready for it.” He cannot foretell which way the country will turn. All he can do now is increase his standing and clout. Melnichenko’s mind still thinks in terms of quantum physics, where the mere act of looking alters the nature of things. “It is only the appearance of an observer that brings sense to the world.” ■
paragraphs.zh: 梅尔尼琴科也明白这一点。他告诉我：“变革时刻无法预测。历史中有些时刻只是发生了。试图加速通常是一种错误。你所能做的，只有观察并为其做好准备。”他无法预言国家将朝哪个方向转向。现在他能做的，也只能是提高自己的地位和影响力。梅尔尼琴科的思维仍然停留在量子物理的范式里，在那里，单纯观察的行为就会改变事物的本质。‘只有观察者的出现，才给世界带来意义。’■

paragraphs.en: Arkady Ostrovsky is The Economist’s Russia editor
paragraphs.zh: 阿卡第·奥斯特洛夫斯基是《经济学人》俄罗斯编辑。
